abstract: We demonstrate that the redox-active metal-organic framework Fe2(dobpdc) (dobpdc4– = 4,4'-dioxidobiphenyl-3,3'-dicarboxylate) undergoes a topotactic oxidative insertion reaction with various weakly coordinating anions, such as BF4– and PF6–. This process occurs with only a minimal contraction of the crystal lattice and triggers the emergence of a broad intervalence charge-transfer band, signaling high electronic mobility within the framework. While the system can undergo both metal- and ligand-based oxidations, we found that only the metal-centered reactions are fully reversible and proceed with high stoichiometric precision under both chemical and electrochemical control. Investigation into the effects of nanoconfinement during these insertion reactions revealed a significant dependence on both the size of the anion and the choice of solvent. Leveraging this reversible anion insertion behavior, we integrated Fe2(dobpdc) into a prototype dual-ion battery featuring a sodium-metal anode. When utilized as a cathode, the framework exhibits an exceptionally high initial reduction potential and maintains stability over at least 50 charge/discharge cycles, achieving a maximum specific energy of 316 Wh/kg.
